title: System Design Primer
description: Core concepts from the System Design Primer and its referenced resources

cards.back: The ability of a system to handle increasing load by adding resources while maintaining acceptable performance.

cards.front: What is the difference between a performance problem and a scalability problem?
cards.back: Performance problems affect a single user under normal load; scalability problems appear as load increases.
The time required to complete a request or operation.
The number of requests or operations completed per unit time.
Adding more CPU, memory, or storage to a single machine.
Adding more machines and distributing load across them.
A component that distributes incoming requests across multiple servers.

cards.front: What are the benefits of load balancing?
cards.back: Improved availability, fault tolerance, scalability, and resource utilization.
A server that sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests.
A Content Delivery Network that caches content closer to users to reduce latency.
They reduce latency, decrease origin server load, and improve availability.
Storing frequently accessed data in faster storage to reduce retrieval time.
When requested data is found in the cache.
When requested data is not found in the cache and must be fetched from a slower source.
Removing entries from a cache when space is needed.

cards.front: What is the LRU cache policy?
cards.back: Least Recently Used; evicts the item that has not been accessed for the longest time.
Writes are sent to both the cache and the database simultaneously.
Writes are stored in cache first and persisted later.
Maintaining copies of data across multiple servers.
To improve availability, fault tolerance, and read scalability.
Writes go to the primary database while replicas serve reads.
Splitting data across multiple databases using a partitioning strategy.
To increase storage capacity and distribute load.
A technique that minimizes data movement when nodes are added or removed.
Dividing data into subsets that can be stored and managed independently.
Intentionally duplicating data to improve read performance.
Organizing relational data to reduce redundancy.
Replicas converge to the same state over time if no new updates occur.
Every read returns the most recent write.

cards.front: What is availability in distributed systems?
cards.back: Every request receives a response, even if it may not contain the latest data.

cards.front: What is consistency in CAP theorem?
cards.back: All nodes see the same data at the same time.
The system continues operating despite network failures between nodes.
A distributed system can only guarantee two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance simultaneously.
A system that prioritizes Consistency and Partition Tolerance over Availability.
A system that prioritizes Availability and Partition Tolerance over immediate Consistency.
A system that enables asynchronous communication between services.
To decouple services, smooth traffic spikes, and improve resilience.
Publish-subscribe messaging where producers send events and subscribers receive them.
Executing work outside the main request-response cycle.
A system whose components run on multiple networked machines and coordinate to achieve a goal.
The ability of a system to continue operating despite failures.
Having duplicate components to eliminate single points of failure.

cards.front: What is a single point of failure?
cards.back: A component whose failure can bring down the entire system.
An application where all functionality is deployed as a single unit.
An independently deployable service focused on a specific business capability.
Independent scaling, deployment flexibility, and team autonomy.
Operational complexity, distributed transactions, and network failures.
A central entry point that routes requests to backend services.
Restricting how many requests a client can make within a time period.
It protects systems from abuse, overload, and denial-of-service attacks.
Performing the same operation multiple times produces the same result.
A mechanism for determining whether a service instance is functioning correctly.
A target level of reliability or performance for a service.
A contractual commitment regarding service reliability or performance.
A cache shared across multiple servers.
A probabilistic data structure used to test whether an element may exist in a set.
The minimum number of nodes required to agree on an operation.

cards.front: What is eventual consistency commonly used for?
cards.back: Large-scale systems where availability and partition tolerance are prioritized.

cards.front: What is a URL shortener interview problem testing?
cards.back: Knowledge of key generation, databases, caching, scaling, and availability.

cards.front: What is the main challenge in designing Twitter timelines?
cards.back: Efficiently generating and delivering feeds for millions of users.

cards.front: What is the main challenge in designing a web crawler?
cards.back: Scalably discovering, fetching, and indexing web content.

cards.front: What is the purpose of system design interviews?
cards.back: To evaluate a candidate's ability to reason about scalability, trade-offs, reliability, and architecture.
